title: Datalab projects during COVID-19 | Stats NZ
content:
## Datalab projects during COVID-19
11 August 2020, 4:00pm
The following key projects were allowed to access the Data Lab remotely during the lockdown.

#### MAA2020-36 Multilayer networks for modelling complex contagion of Covid-19 in New Zealand University of Auckland
Shaun Hendy Dion O’Neale
Outline This project will build an individual based network that is representative of the NZ population, including schools, workplaces, family structure, community groups, movements between and within cities, etc. It will use this network as the basis to run detailed complex contagion models including spatial and occupational effects for the COVID epidemic, under different scenarios.
Specific areas of focus include, for example, giving a detailed prediction of likely contagion spread if certain industry sectors had reduced restrictions; if different geographic regions moved to different levels at different times; or how different working arrangements might affect contagion outcomes, including for different demographic groups. These will be compared with the expected contagion, in the case of no control. The key to this is that we will be using data for the NZ population from the Stats NZ IDI to parameterise our networks, combined with what we know about the topological properties of real-world networks.
Use This project was regularly submitting the outputs from the data lab directly to the National Crisis Management Centre.

#### MAA2015-22 Delivering a new measure of neighbourhood disadvantage for New Zealand University of Auckland
Outline Variation Name : MAA2015-22-12- Using the existing geo-spatial analyses from the MAA2015-22 project, we will be contributing to essential COVID -19 research to assist in the mitigation of the ongoing pandemic.
Professor Chris Bullen, a member of the Ministry of Health's COVID-19 Technical Advisory Group is leading this study, which comprises a team of researchers from team of researchers from the University of Auckland and University of Otago. However, we are only requesting access to the IDI data lab for the members of the Auckland-based analytical team, who will report results to the wider team when data have been output.
Use Supporting the Ministry of Health’s COVID-19 Technical Advisory Group

#### MAA2015-27 IDI Based micro-simulation modelling of the New Zealand tax and welfare system Treasury
Outline This project uses the IDI to complement survey information with linked admin data – this complemented information is the main input into our tax and welfare microsimulation model TAWA. TAWA is used extensively to model the impacts of tax and welfare policies (including impacts on child poverty). This model will prove critical should the situation with COVID 19 worsen, because the Government will have to consider more extensive measures (and their impacts) in order to support the New Zealand economy. Substantial modelling may be required to identify the most effective policy and its impact.
Use Model outputs were used to inform Treasury’s policy advice on the response to COVID.

#### MAA2013-16 Citizen Pathways Treasury
Outline Information was sought to understand the fiscal and wider implications of potential school closures, by region and nationally.
Use The analysis was used to inform Treasury’s policy advice on the response to COVID.

#### MAA2011-01 Firm behaviour and linkages to economic and fiscal policy Treasury
Outline Variation Name : MAA2011-01-4- Information on businesses is being sought by the TSY policy team working on the governments Covid19 response. Variation Name : MAA2011-01-5- Investigate the impacts of the Covid-19 outbreak and the subsequent lockdown measures in NZ on businesses and their employees.
It is important for us to identify what areas of the economy have been impacted by the lockdown so we can help inform decisions about future recovery policies or interventions.
Use The analysis was used to inform Treasury’s policy advice on the response to COVID.

#### MAA2019-49 Behaviour and performance of New Zealand firms Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment
Outline Variation Name : MAA2019-49-8- The new question fits under the ‘sector profiles’ part of the Datalab agreement.
This question seeks to understand the scope and scale of the potential economic impacts of the coronavirus outbreak. The focus will be on firms in industries with heavy trade exposure to China.
Variation Name : MAA2019-49-7- This new research questions fits under the broad intention of the Datalab agreement to conduct research to understand the performance of New Zealand firms.
The new research question seeks to explore the potential impacts of rising carbon prices on firms and workers in NZ. We will look at the industries most exposed to rising carbon prices and provide a demographic profile of these industries to understand how rising carbon prices may impact different groups of firms and workers in NZ.
Variation Name : MAA2019-49-10- How well are the New Zealand government’s support measures working to mitigate the negative effects of COVID-19 on firms?
- What are the characteristics of firms who have accessed support, including: - firm demographics such as sector, size, region, ownership structure - long term prospects for success post-COVID
- Is support getting to the firms most at risk of failure?
- How have firms used the support they have received?
- How are the firms who have accessed support faring, in terms of: - staff retention? - staffing hours? - cashflow? - liquidity? - other factors?
- What has happened to firms who have not accessed the support?
Use Directly feeding into Ministerial advice for NCMC and government response

#### MAA207-60 Education system performance for preschool and school age children Ministry of Education
Outline Variation Name : MAA2017-60-24- Our new research question is: ‘What schools would we need to keep open during the lockdown, given a list of essential workers, and their school-aged children?’
We need to determine which schools need to be open during the Covid-19 lockdown to support the students whose parents are considered employees of essential businesses and services. We have done preliminary work to identify the schools in proximity to hospitals, however at the moment, these analyses are done with old (or incomplete) data, or with estimation. If we were able to access the IDI, we could provide more precise and useful evidence to our leadership, so that we could better support our kids on these short turnarounds we are faced with. We believe access to micro-level IRD data will help us better identify essential workers
Use Ministry of Education response to school support during and after lockdown.

#### MAA2016-23 Vulnerable Children Investment Approach Microdata Project Oranga Tamariki
Outline Variation Name : MAA2016-23 - understanding the likely impact of the current situation on all New Zealand children’s wellbeing It is critical to understand the potential short-term impact of Covid-19 and Alert Levels 3 and 4 on child and family wellbeing, but also longer-term impacts driven by, for instance, increased financial stress. This work is designed to support the immediate response as well as the longer-term recovery planning, and will include the following as required:
- Modelling within the IDI using the Children’s Wellbeing Model to assess the impacts on child and family wellbeing, including impacts on family violence, household resource security, unemployment and benefit dependency to name a few
- The extraction of information to inform non-IDI-based impact analyses, including information on the key Oranga Tamariki populations; Early Intervention, Intensive Intervention, Care, Youth Justice, and Transitions.
- Examination of vulnerable sub-populations, such as children with disabilities.
- Extraction of Māori and iwi-specific information
Use Initial publication will be limited to in-house summaries and reports to be provided to the:
- Oranga Tamariki Incident Management Team
- Oranga Tamariki Leadership Team
- Minister for Children, the Honourable Tracey Martin
- All of Government response groups
Where applicable, results will be shared with related agencies to support the all-of-government work. For instance, outputs of our modelling may feed into modelling work conducted by the Ministry for Social Development, Ministry of Health, Ministry of Education, and the Ministry of Justice.
